name: Beastly
classification_notes: A display typeface rooted in Herb Lubalin-era lettering, with tightly spaced, optically scaled letterforms and characteristically heavy, expressive serifs typical of 1970s graphic design.
summary: A Lubalin-era display serif originally commissioned for the Lubalin 100 centenary project, offered in nine optical sizes.
description: Designed as a custom typeface for the Lubalin 100 project with art director Sasha Tochilovsky, Beastly draws on the lettering traditions of Herb Lubalin's studio without directly aping any single existing face. Nine optical sizes from 12 Point to 72 Point each carry subtly adjusted spacing and weight to suit their intended setting, from running text to large-scale poster work. Named after a Vulfpeck track from their 2011 debut EP, it sits comfortably in editorial, packaging and poster contexts that want the energy of 1970s New York graphic design.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Beastly as a riff on Lubalin-era typography, originally created as a custom typeface for the Lubalin 100 project. Rather than copying an existing face, the design aimed to capture the spirit of Lubalin's lettering team while producing something new. The family ships in nine optical sizes, with features including hairline accents, hairline punctuation, fractions, subscripts and numerators.

name: Camelion
classification_notes: Camelion is described as a humanist text face with origins in slanted roman and upright italic constructions, reflecting Nugue's humanist drawing sensibility. The warmth and legibility at text sizes, combined with chiselled forms, place it firmly in humanist serif territory.
summary: A humanist text face by Sandrine Nugue that inverts conventional roman and italic roles, pairing chiselled clarity with warmth at paragraph sizes.
description: Designed by Sandrine Nugue and released through OH no Type in 2024, Camelion subverts typographic convention by rooting itself in the slanted roman and upright italic tradition rather than the standard pairing. Four weights from Regular to ExtraBold, each with a matching italic, make up eight styles. The result is a text face that reads with high legibility while retaining the hand and humanity that characterise Nugue's broader body of work.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Camelion as a distillation of Sandrine Nugue's signature drawing acumen and bias towards humanist shapes into a razor-sharp text face. James Edmondson notes its origins in the slanted roman and upright italic tradition, calling it clear and chiselled but never cold, adding that he loves it precisely because he would never have drawn it himself.

name: Casserole
classification_notes: Casserole is built on the bones of Davida, a heavily ball-terminal display face from 1966, and expands it into six stylistically distinct subfamilies including blackletter, script and Lombardic, all united by the same exuberant ball-terminal aesthetic.
summary: A six-subfamily revival and expansion of Louis Minott's 1966 display face Davida, united by its signature ball terminals across Classic, Sans, Flare, Script, Blackletter and Lombardic styles.
description: OH no Type describe it as an exhaustive resuscitation of Davida, pushing the ball-terminal logic of the original into wildly divergent territory. Each of the six styles shares the same decorative DNA while offering a completely different register, from the stripped-back Sans to the ornate Lombardic. OpenType features include intelligent swash-crossbar removal, non-lining figures, case-sensitive punctuation, fractions and a set of border and textile ornaments.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type bill Casserole as six flavours all in the same dish: an exhaustive resuscitation of Davida, the 1966 certified banger from Louis Minott. The family spans Classic, Sans, Flare, Script, Blackletter and Lombardic, and OH no are quick to note they're not asking you to like it, just to respect it.

name: Cheee
classification_notes: A wildly expressive variable display typeface with axes for Yeast and Gravity, producing dripping, wobbly letterforms across 25 named styles. Clearly display category with no conventional serif or sans-serif classification applicable.
summary: A variable display typeface with axes for Yeast and Gravity, producing dripping, blobby letterforms across 25 named styles with roots in 1970s psychedelia.
description: Cheee uses two variable axes, Yeast and Gravity, to morph letterforms from tightly controlled to wildly dripping globs, with a companion Small style for subheads. The 25 named styles, each named after real people bar Bingbong, span the full range of the axes and can be purchased individually or as a complete family including a single variable font file. Production work was contributed by Alexis Boscariol, and the design openly acknowledges its debt to 1970s vernacular lettering.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Cheee as offering six levels of Yeast, four levels of Gravity, and a bonus Small style for subheads across 25 styles and one variable font. They invite users to draw their own drips, warp text to curves, and let go of the baseline entirely, framing the family as overkill by design.

name: Compadre
classification_notes: An all-caps display typeface offered in four widths from Narrow to Extended, designed explicitly to pair with other display faces rather than stand alone as a text face.
summary: An all-caps, single-weight typeface in four widths, built specifically to complement and glue together other display faces in the OH no Type library.
description: Capitals only, four widths ranging from Narrow to Extended, and a single weight: Compadre is deliberately plain, designed to sit alongside more expressive display faces without competing. OH no Type position it as the connective tissue of their library, and it ships with pairing suggestions for Swear, Hobeaux, Blazeface and others. At $20 per width or $59 for the complete family, it is priced as a workhorse companion rather than a headline act.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Compadre as the capitals-only typeface that acts as the glue of the entire Ohno library, offering four widths from Narrow to Extended with just enough personality to sit comfortably alongside your favourite display types.

name: Coniferous
classification_notes: A connected script with seamless letter joins and swash caps, directly modelled on the hand-lettered signage of American National Forests. The style sits within casual or informal script traditions.
summary: A connected script modelled on the hand-lettered signage used at American National Forest entrances, available in six weights with swash caps and a variable font.
description: Built from the routed yellow script of US National Forest signs, the family runs from Thin to Black with seamless letter connections and a set of alternate swash capitals for display use. OpenType features cover contextual substitution, ligatures, bouncing caps and fractions, extending its range beyond purely outdoor or heritage-themed work. A variable font is included alongside the six individual static styles.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Coniferous as a tribute to childhood memories of driving to Sequoia National Park, where the National Forest signage signalled the start of holiday. The typeface reproduces those routed letterforms across six weights with seamless connections, swash caps, sparkles and full OpenType feature support, with a variable font also available.

name: Covik Sans
classification_notes: A proportional grotesque with brushy, humanist terminals informed by its serif origins, alongside a monospaced variant with quantized character widths. The forms lean humanist rather than geometric, with deliberate terminal treatment for text legibility.
summary: A humanist sans originating as a TypeMedia graduation project, refined through a custom commission for a restaurant tasting menu before being developed into a text-ready family with a companion monospaced variant.
description: Covik Sans grew from a TypeMedia graduation project, stripped of serifs and expanded for practical use after a bespoke commission for a fine-dining test kitchen. The proportional family runs in five weights with italics, each terminal deliberately flattened to produce a subtle sparkle in running text. Covik Sans Mono extends the family with a quantized three-width system that softens the rhythmic disruption of conventional monospacing, switchable between faux and true mono via OpenType.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Covik Sans as a graduation project that pivoted away from expressive display type toward a sober text sans, later refined through a custom commission before being released as a retail family. The mono variant was developed for Future Fonts' UI, using a quantized width system to balance the quirk of monospacing with everyday legibility, with a true mono OpenType feature added on suggestion from a collaborator.

name: Degular
classification_notes: Degular is a geometric-leaning sans-serif designed explicitly to be unremarkable and neutral, with three optical sizes (Display, Text, and standard) and a monospaced companion, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A deliberately unremarkable geometric sans by OH no Type, offered across three optical sizes and a monospaced variant, totalling 56 styles with variable font support.
description: Designed to recede rather than attract attention, Degular is a neutral geometric sans available in seven weights with roman and italic across Display, standard and Text optical cuts. A monospaced variant, Degular Mono, extends the family for code and technical settings, while variable fonts are included with full family purchases. The three optical sizes allow a single typographic voice to move from large-scale display work down to small running text without adjustment.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type pitch Degular as the anti-attention-seeker in their catalogue, a font built to fade into the background rather than compete for eyeballs. It comes in 7 weights, 3 optical sizes, roman and italic, plus a monospaced companion that they describe as Degular doing a robot voice. The full complete package spans 56 styles and two variable fonts, with OpenType features including multiple figure styles, small caps, alternates, fractions, arrows, and box-drawing glyphs in the mono.

name: Eckmannpsych
classification_notes: A psychedelia-inflected reworking of Otto Eckmann's Art Nouveau blackletter-adjacent display typeface, placing it firmly in the display category with Jugendstil roots and deliberate distortion.
summary: A psychedelia-inflected revival of Otto Eckmann's Art Nouveau display lettering, issued in three optical sizes designed to maintain consistent thin strokes across scales.
description: Built on the ornate, organic letterforms of Otto Eckmann's late-nineteenth-century typeface, this version layers in deliberate distortion and hallucinatory character to create something between archival revival and graphic provocation. Three optical sizes, Small, Medium and Large, allow the characteristic hairlines to remain consistent whether set as body copy or at poster scale. Bizarre umlaut variants, overlappable styles for instant psychedelic layering, and a registered trademark that took long enough to draw it needed registering make this a genuinely characterful display family.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Eckmannpsych as a psychedelia-infused version of Eckmann that sat dormant until a friend requested a customisation with additional letters. The family comprises three optical sizes designed to enable consistent thin strokes, with features including alternate characters, layerable styles for psychedelic overlay effects, and umlaut forms inspired by original Otto Eckmann drawings.

name: Forevs
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as potentially glyphic or incised with classical references; the design draws from Art Nouveau letterforms and historic sources, placing it in a glyphic or lapidary serif tradition with distinctive historical personality.
summary: A glyphic serif by James Edmondson begun in a 90-minute garage session in 2022, drawing from Art Nouveau, incised and classical lettering traditions across seven weights.
description: James Edmondson sketched the original uppercase in a single 90-minute sitting in Oakland in June 2022, and the family has since grown to 14 styles spanning Thin to Super with matching italics and two variable fonts. The design sits somewhere between glyphic, incised and classical traditions, with an extensive set of alternates referencing Art Nouveau, Lombardic forms and historic models including Marlowe-style R variants and high-waisted Fs. It has appeared in branding, editorial and poster work, including a school identity by Wonderhood Studios and a Hinge campaign.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Forevs as a typeface that resists easy classification, inviting comparisons to glyphic, incised and classical styles. The family comprises seven weights with roman and italic pairs across 14 styles, plus two variable fonts, and comes loaded with OpenType alternates drawing on Art Nouveau, Lombardic and other historical lettering sources.

name: Hobeaux
classification_notes: A revival of the 1910 Hobo typeface, itself an art nouveau design with no descenders and distinctive curved baselines. The display category is appropriate given its expressive, decorative character and limited suitability for body text.
summary: A thorough revival of the 1910 Hobo typeface, redrawn from scratch across five weights with expanded characters and OpenType features the original never had.
description: Every character was redrawn and respaced from scratch, correcting the degraded curves that accumulated through decades of technology transitions and a careless early digitisation. The family adds stylistic alternates for descending letters, ornaments, burst figures, arrows and other characters that were never drawn for the original Hobo. Five weights from Light to Black make it more versatile than the single-weight Hobo most designers know from pre-installed system fonts.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Hobeaux as a modern revival of the 1910 art nouveau Hobo, which degraded through successive type technology transitions and received a poor early digitisation in the 1980s. Each character was redrawn and spaced from scratch, with descending letter alternates added as stylistic options alongside ornaments, burst figures and other characters absent from the original. The family spans five weights and includes several OpenType features for designers wanting a high-performance version of the typeface.

name: Hobeaux Rococeaux
classification_notes: A highly ornate all-caps display typeface derived from Paul Carlyle's Carlyle Roccoco, intended exclusively for large-size settings with decorative layering and border elements. Its intricate linework and lack of lowercase firmly place it in the ornamental display category.
summary: An ornate all-caps display typeface by OH no Type, rooted in Paul Carlyle's Carlyle Roccoco, with layering, border and small companion styles built for short settings at large sizes.
description: Derived from Paul Carlyle's intricately ornate Carlyle Roccoco, originally designed for Photolettering in the 1970s, this family packages foreground, background, border and compact Sherman styles into a single chromatic system. Individual glyphs took between one and three hours to draw, and the Borders font comes with a Drawbot script for generating circular and repeating ornamental patterns. Suited to packaging, signage and short-run display work where maximum decorative impact is the point.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Hobeaux Rococeaux as a hybrid of Carlyle Roccoco and Hobeaux, inspired by the ornate typefaces Paul Carlyle designed while working for Photolettering in the seventies. The scope is intentionally small, with no lowercase and a single weight, designed for short settings at large sizes. The family includes a background layer for multicolour use, a borders font usable in any graphics application or with Drawbot, and Sherman, a compact companion named after type historian and designer Nick Sherman.

name: Irregardless
classification_notes: An experimental display typeface with a deliberately unsettling and unconventional design, sitting outside conventional grotesque or humanist classifications. The Containers style and unusual OpenType features push it firmly into display territory.
summary: A deliberately uncomfortable display typeface from OH no Type, built across four weights with italics, an oddball Containers dingbat style, and a variable font thrown in.
description: Designed as an exercise in resisting instinct and familiar drawing habits, this typeface sits at the weirder end of OH no Type's catalogue. Four weights run from Light to Black with matching italics, and the Containers style provides stackable dingbats and alternative letterforms for typographic embellishment. Stylistic sets offer heart-dotted i's, alternate narrow and wide forms, and non-lining figures, making it well-suited to packaging, beer labels and editorial work where strangeness is an asset.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Irregardless as existing at the opposite end of the design spectrum from a clear-cut brief. Designer James Edmondson notes he had no idea where it came from or where it was going, framing it as a deliberate fight against impulse and habit to produce something new and uncomfortable. The family includes four weights in roman and italic, a Containers dingbat style, and a variable font included with purchase of the full family.

name: Montreuil
designer: Julien Priez, James Edmondson, Colin M. Ford, Jamie Otelsberg
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque sans, drawn by a calligrapher with a subtle humanist flavour in the lowercase letterforms, particularly the a and t, which keeps it from being a pure geometric or neo-grotesque.
summary: A grotesque sans with a subtle humanist inflection, drawn over fifteen years as a collaboration between OH no Type and French calligrapher-designer Julien Priez, issued in seven weights with italics and an experimental modular Play style.
description: Conceived after James Edmondson persuaded Priez to approach a grotesque despite his calligraphic background, the family carries just enough personality in its lowercase a and t to distinguish it from generic grotesques without compromising legibility. Fifteen styles in total cover everything from logotype to extended text setting, with the single-style Play variant offering a modular kit of parts for experimental composition. The variable font covers the full weight range, and a generous OpenType feature set includes small caps, multiple figure sets and case-sensitive punctuation.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Montreuil as part experiment, part stalwart: a text family with the proportions and quality of a world-class release alongside an experimental Play counterpart built from modular parts. Drawn between 2010 and 2025 by Julien Priez, James Edmondson, Colin M. Ford and Jamie Otelsberg across France, Germany and the United States, it is positioned to handle every job from logotype to encyclopedia.

name: Obviously
classification_notes: A wide-ranging grotesque family drawing from the tradition of American commercial display types, impact-style ultra-condensed faces and extended vinyl-sign lettering. The breadth of widths from Compressed to Extended puts it firmly in the display grotesque camp.
summary: A 96-style grotesque spanning six widths and eight weights, conceived from vinyl signage, Impact stretched to absurdity and hand-painted commercial lettering.
description: Built across six widths from Compressed to Extended and eight weights from Thin to Super, the family gives equal attention to the squat, peeling vinyl billboard and the expansive painted shopfront. Each width ships with a full roman and italic set, and a variable font covers the entire range including a semislant axis unavailable in the static cuts. OpenType features include alternate characters for A, G, T and Y, lining and non-lining figures, case-sensitive punctuation and fractions.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Obviously as a love letter to vinyl signage, stretched Impact and Antique Olive Nord squished to comic proportions, designed for nail salons, laundromats and billboard CONS printed in black on obnoxious neon. The family runs to 96 styles across six widths and eight weights in roman and italic, with a variable font also available. Subtlety, they say, is for the birds.

name: Ohno Blazeface
classification_notes: A fatface display serif with extreme stroke contrast, sitting firmly in the display/fatface tradition but deliberately redrawn to minimise internal whitespace and push problematic letterforms into unconventional solutions.
summary: A nine-optical-size fatface serif that pushes the genre by minimising internal whitespace and finding unconventional solutions for letters that typically defeat the style.
description: Where most fatfaces leave awkward voids in letters like f, x and z, Blazeface takes an unorthodox approach to each, filling positive space as aggressively as possible. Nine optical sizes run from 12pt through to 72pt, each available in Roman and Italic, with the italic adding teardrop-terminated hairlines that give words a pronounced roundness. A full set of ornaments, arrows, fractions and snowflakes accompanies the 18 styles.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Blazeface as a real fatty of a display serif, born from frustration with the fatface genre's inability to handle problematic letters and its consistently traditional structure. The italic is positioned as a companion with its own personality, exaggerating entry and exit strokes into subtle teardrop shapes and solving the usual whitespace problems in f, x and z through unusual letterforms.

name: Ohno Casual
designer: James Edmondson, Corinne Ang, Jamie Otelsberg, Colin Ford
classification_notes: Rooted in the sign-painting tradition of single-stroke casual lettering, but with an unconventional variable design space combining weight and contrast axes that push it firmly into display territory rather than workaday script.
summary: A sign-painting-inspired casual with an unusual variable design space that pairs a weight axis with a contrast axis, producing a diagonal trajectory through the design space.
description: Drawn by James Edmondson with Corinne Ang, Jamie Otelsberg and Colin Ford, Ohno Casual takes the single-stroke efficiency of traditional sign-painting casuals and deliberately undermines its rationalism. The variable font combines a weight axis with a contrast axis in a diagonal design space, and the static family runs six weights from Thin to Black. A companion Textile font handles ornamental and dingbat material, and squiggly alternates sit alongside case-sensitive punctuation and non-lining figures throughout.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Ohno Casual as rooted in the sign-painting tradition of single-stroke casual lettering, efficient and ergonomic by nature, but deliberately loaded with choices that resist easy justification. The variable font features both a weight and contrast axis arranged diagonally across the design space, which they flag as non-standard, alongside squiggly alternates, a dingbat companion font, and an intentionally tiny dot on the i. Released in 2022, designed by James Edmondson, Corinne Ang, Jamie Otelsberg and Colin Ford.

name: Ohno Fatface
classification_notes: A high-contrast display serif in the tradition of early 19th-century fatface types from the Fann Street foundry, extended across five widths and nine optical sizes for contemporary use.
summary: A 45-style fatface family with five widths and nine optical sizes, tracing a direct line from early 19th-century Fann Street display types to contemporary editorial and poster work.
description: Built across five widths — Normal, Narrow, Condensed, Compressed and Squished — each with nine optical sizes, the family adjusts stroke contrast and spacing as sizes decrease so the type remains functional well below headline scale. A variable font is included with the full package, making the width and optical-size axes accessible for layout work. The design was drawn between 2017 and 2020 and sits squarely in the revival of 1970s-influenced fatface aesthetics that defined much of that period's typographic output.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Ohno Fatface as rooted in the fatface genre pioneered by the Fann Street foundry in the early 1800s, originally conceived for short words printed large. They extend that premise into a full 45-style family spanning five widths and nine optical sizes, with a variable font included in the complete package, describing it as a Swiss Army knife for all fatface needs. The family was designed between 2017 and 2020.

name: Ohno Softie
designer: James Edmondson, Jamie Otelsberg, and Colin Ford
classification_notes: A geometric sans built entirely around circular terminals and rounded negative shapes, pushing the soft-edged rounded genre to an extreme with fully circular counters and ends throughout.
summary: A rounded geometric sans from OH no Type that takes fully circular terminals and negative shapes to their logical extreme across five weights.
description: Designed by James Edmondson, Jamie Otelsberg and Colin Ford in 2022, every stroke end and interior counter is rounded, giving words a consistently soft, syrupy texture that most rounded sans faces only approximate. Five weights run from a near-elegant Light through to a full-bodied Black, with a variable font included in the complete family. OpenType features include alternate double-storey 'a', alternate 'f' and 't' without left crossbars, case-sensitive punctuation and fractions.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Ohno Softie as the answer for when even the most rounded geometric sans still feels too harsh, with every terminal and every negative shape made circular. Five voluptuous weights cater for everything from chic elegance at Light to full-blown cosy warmth at Black, and the foundry singles out fabric softener brand designers as a natural audience.

name: Polymath
designer: James Edmondson, Colin M. Ford, Jamie Otelsberg
classification_notes: Polymath draws directly from the geometric sans tradition, citing Paul Renner's Futura as a primary reference. Its circular forms and constructed letterforms place it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory, though extensive alternates allow it to move between strict geometric and more humanist registers.
summary: A geometric sans in three optical sizes with nine weights, extensive OpenType alternates and a variable font, designed by James Edmondson, Colin M. Ford and Jamie Otelsberg between 2021 and 2024.
description: Developed as a more neutral counterpart to OH no Type's Degular, Polymath takes the geometric sans genre as its starting point with clear Futura lineage, then adds a wide array of stylistic alternates ranging from legibility forms and double-storey a and g, to Bauhaus-influenced characters, gaspipe variants and an uncial-style a. Three optical sizes cover running text, general-purpose setting and large-scale display work, with all nine weights available in roman and italic across each cut. The complete family runs to 54 styles plus a variable font, making it one of the more fully realised geometric sans releases of recent years.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Polymath as a geometric sans ready for use at any size, spanning three optical sizes for paragraphs, headlines and everything in between. Drawn between 2021 and 2024, it takes artful inspiration from Futura and channels that through an extensive set of stylistic alternates, including double-storey forms, Bauhausian variants, gaspipe alternates and more unusual options like a squiggly g and uncial a. Small caps, multiple figure sets and Vietnamese support round out the feature set.

name: Regrets
classification_notes: A decorative display type with explicit Art Nouveau influence, featuring organic ornamental details, floral motifs and contextual alternates typical of the late 19th-century revival aesthetic.
summary: An Art Nouveau-influenced display family in two weights, accompanied by a standalone ornaments font and a vector borders file.
description: Drawing on the lineage of Arnold Bocklin and Mario Feliciano's Korrodi, this decorative family distils Art Nouveau's organic character into two degrees: Major and Minor. Contextual alternates include straight-legged R and K, flat lowercase stems, a flagged R and a flat-sided E, giving typographers room to control how much period flair appears on the page. The Ornaments font supplies floral and tile patterns, and a bonus vector file of borders rounds out the package.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Regrets as a two-degree Art Nouveau-influenced display family comprising Major, Minor and an Ornaments font, with a bonus vector borders file. The designer references Arnold Bocklin and Korrodi by Mario Feliciano as touchstones, framing the family as a more subtle evolution of their decorative work since Beastly and Retail.

name: Swear
classification_notes: Swear is a high-contrast serif with exaggerated razor-thin exterior serifs and a pronounced weight differential, developed from flat-brush calligraphic exploration. Its four optical sizes and expressive italic variants place it firmly in the display-to-text serif tradition.
summary: A high-contrast serif spanning four optical sizes and six weights, with a reverse italic called Cilati as a second emphasis option alongside a conventional italic.
description: Swear runs from Text to Banner across 72 styles, combining Romans, Italics and the inverted 'Cilati' cuts into a full system for everything from running copy to large-format headings. The extreme contrast came from flat-brush practice, with exterior serifs pushed to razor thinness and interior serifs kept deliberately large to manage spacing on letters like A and V. Variable fonts for each of the three stylistic axes are included with the complete package.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Swear as 'familiar flagrancy for your agency,' comprising 6 weights across 4 optical sizes in Roman, Italic and the reverse italic Cilati for a total of 72 styles. The family grew out of the designer's display-first approach, with text sizes derived from display origins, and features small caps, multiple figure sets, fractions, arrows and variable font files included in the complete package.

name: Viktor Script
classification_notes: A wet-brush script with expressive, hand-painted letterforms; the caps variant confirms its script classification with an all-capitals brush style as a companion.
summary: A wet-brush script designed in collaboration with lettering artist Erik Marinovich, with a lively, paint-still-wet quality and an all-caps companion style.
description: Created with lettering artist Erik Marinovich, Viktor Script carries the loose energy of hand-painted brush lettering, including alternate characters, speech bubbles, dingbats and skulls wearing hats. The family is two styles: a lowercase-and-caps script and an all-capitals variant sold separately. Suited to packaging, campaign work and anything that calls for exuberant, hand-done warmth.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Viktor Script as a collaboration with Erik Marinovich, emphasising its brushy, paint-still-wet character and emotional, joyful quality. The family includes alternates, dingbats, arrows, speech bubbles and other extras across two styles: Regular and Caps.

name: Vulf
classification_notes: Vulf Mono draws directly from the IBM Selectric typewriter's 12 Point Light Italic, placing it firmly in the typewriter-inspired monospace tradition. Vulf Sans is a proportionally spaced derivative, but the family's identity and origin are rooted in the mono.
summary: A typewriter-inspired monospace and its slab-stripped sans companion, designed as the official typeface of funk band Vulfpeck.
description: Vulf Mono takes its cues from the IBM Selectric's 12 Point Light Italic, rendered across four weights from Light to Black with matching italics, amounting to eight styles and 434 glyphs per style. Vulf Sans emerged from the same source by removing the slab serifs and switching to proportional spacing, adding a Medium weight to produce ten styles. Both families carry an unmistakable warmth and rhythmic irregularity that suits editorial, music and identity work where character is more valuable than neutrality.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describe Vulf Mono as the official typeface of Vulfpeck, the funky four-piece rhythm section from Ann Arbor, Michigan, with its primary inspiration drawn from the IBM Selectric typewriter's 12 Point Light Italic. Vulf Sans followed as a second collaboration, described as the result of chopping off the slab serifs and making it proportionally spaced — simple in concept but not in execution. The complete package spans 18 styles, includes music dingbats, arrows, lining and non-lining figures, and supports over 200 languages.

name: Yumex
classification_notes: Yumex is a geometric-leaning sans-serif with a mid-century sensibility, paired with a companion script family that together reference 1950s–60s Yugoslav and Mexican pop culture graphics.
summary: A mid-century sans and script pairing by Zrinka Buljubašić and Gen Ramírez, inspired by the unlikely cultural crossover between Yugoslav and Mexican pop graphics of the 1950s and 60s.
description: Yumex comes in two families: a geometric sans in seven weights with roman and italic cuts, and a companion script in seven weights, totalling 21 styles and two variable fonts. The concept draws on the period when Yugoslavia, unable to import Soviet or American films, developed a genuine enthusiasm for Mexican cinema, spawning graphic material that boldly combined sans-serifs with expressive scripts. The result is a type family with a specific and well-researched cultural premise, as useful for editorial and display work as it is for identity projects that want warmth and period flavour.
foundry_blurb: OH no Type describes Yumex as a celebration of the strange love between Yugoslavian modernism and mid-century Mexican pop culture, rooted in the 1950s–60s phenomenon of Yugoslav audiences embracing Mexican movies, music and imagery when Soviet and American imports were off the table. The family pairs a bold geometric sans with a companion script, both available in seven weights, reflecting the graphic language of that era where bold sans-serifs and expressive scripts appeared side by side across music, cinema and cultural life.
